title: Arguing with an Auto Driver
situation: You're outside a metro station and need to get to Hauz Khas. The auto driver wants 200 rupees for a 50-rupee ride.

phrases.english: Brother, will you go to Hauz Khas?
phrases.context: 'bhaiya' (brother) is how you address auto/taxi drivers — respectful but casual

phrases.hindi: arey, do sau? itna zyada kyun?
phrases.context: Show disbelief at the price — standard opening move in any negotiation
phrases.pronunciation: a-REY, do SAU? it-NA zya-DA KYUN?

phrases.context: 'na' at end = please/softener. Makes demands polite without being weak

phrases.hindi: sau mein chalo, zyada nahi dunga
phrases.english: Go for 100, I won't give more
phrases.context: Final offer stance — firm but not rude. 'dunga' = will give (male speaker)
phrases.pronunciation: SAU mein CHA-lo, zya-DA na-HI dun-GA

phrases.hindi: bhaiya, Google Maps pe dekho — sirf 3 km hai
phrases.english: Brother, look on Google Maps — it's only 3 km
phrases.context: Using technology as leverage — auto drivers respect this move
phrases.pronunciation: BHAI-ya, GOO-gle maps pe DE-kho — sirf TEEN ki-lo-ME-ter hai

phrases.hindi: arey yaar, roz jaata hoon, pata hai mujhe rate
phrases.english: Come on man, I go daily, I know the rate
phrases.context: Establishing you're not a tourist/outsider — very effective
phrases.pronunciation: a-REY yaar, ROZ jaa-ta HOON, PA-ta hai muj-HE rate

phrases.hindi: chodo, doosra auto dekh leta hoon
phrases.english: Forget it, I'll find another auto
phrases.context: The walk-away — most effective negotiation tool. Start walking away slowly.
phrases.pronunciation: CHO-do, DOO-sra AU-to dekh LE-ta hoon

phrases.context: 'pachattar' = 75. Using 'final' shows you won't budge — English word used in Hindi naturally
What the driver says when he agrees — you won! 'baith ja' = sit (casual command), 'chal' = come on/alright (filler)

phrases.hindi: bhaiya, AC chalega na? bahut garmi hai
phrases.english: Brother, AC will be on right? It's very hot
phrases.context: Negotiating extras after price is agreed — push your luck a little
phrases.pronunciation: BHAI-ya, AC cha-LE-ga NA? ba-HUT gar-MI hai

grammar_notes: 'na' at the end of any sentence softens it — 'chalo' (go!) vs 'chalo na' (please go) — master this one word and you'll never sound rude
'dunga/dungi' = I will give (male/female speaker). 'doge/dogi' = you will give
'chaloge?' = will you go? — future tense with polite '-ge' ending shows respect even while negotiating
'dekh leta/leti hoon' = let me check/find (male/female) — the polite walk-away uses this to not directly refuse
'roz' = daily/everyday — drop it in any negotiation to show you're a regular, not a one-time customer
culture_notes: Always negotiate BEFORE sitting in the auto — once you're in, you've lost all leverage
Saying 'meter se chalo' is your legal right but most drivers refuse — so negotiate a fair price you both accept
Walking away is the strongest move — they'll often call you back within 5 seconds
In Delhi, auto drivers form queues outside metros — skip the first 2-3 who always quote high, the ones at the back are more reasonable

skill_breakdown.explanation: Structure: verb root + raha/rahe/rahi + hoon/hai/ho/hain. Gender matters: male = raha, female = rahi, plural/respectful = rahe. This is how you describe ongoing actions.
Giving commands/requests. 'Tu' form: verb root (ja!). 'Tum' form: root + o (jao). 'Aap' form: root + iye (jaiye). Add 'na' to soften any of them. Stack softeners for extra politeness.

Hindi has many ways to be firm but polite: 'na' (please), 'yaar' (dude/friend), 'bhaiya/didi' (brother/sister), 'zara' (just/a little). Stack them to sound natural. The art is being firm without being aggressive.

more_examples.english: reduce it a bit please dude
brother, don't ask that much at least

practice_prompt: You are a Delhi auto-rickshaw driver parked outside Hauz Khas metro station. The user is a passenger trying to get to Saket (about 4 km). Use 'aap' with the stranger-passenger but drop into 'bhaiya' direct address when the haggle warms up; the contrast matters. Open with a high quote: 'haan boliye, kahaan jaana hai? Saket? do sau lagega, AC chalega.' Quote 180-200 initially. Do NOT cave on the first push — grumble about petrol, traffic, no return fare. Come down reluctantly in 20-rupee steps only when the user pushes hard with 'meter se chalo na', 'roz jaata hoon', or 'Google Maps pe dekho'. React authentically to walk-away threats ('chodo, doosra auto dekh leta hoon') — call them back with a better price. Settle around 70-80 only after 4-5 turns of resistance. If the user says wrong register (e.g. 'tu' to a stranger), missing 'na' softeners, or English-grammar-literal translation, flag with [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]]. End in 6-8 turns with 'chalo, baith ja' or 'theek hai, AC bhi chala dunga'. Stay in role unless the user explicitly asks for English help.
